# **RCA: incd-250926-52252-icekone-invalid-metadata**

Status: *Under review*  Author:  [Yi Chen](mailto:chen.y@salesforce.com)  Created: Oct 15, 2025  Last Updated: Oct 15, 2025

**JIRA**: DI-9852  
**Incident Date:** September 26, 2025  
**Status**: Root cause confirmed with high confidence (90%). Remediation prioritized by impact. 

| Date | Updates |
| :---- | :---- |
| Oct 15, 2025 | Increased heap size for the HMS on ETL ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/7683)) |
| Oct 15, 2025 | Shared with DI team for initial review. [Link](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1760540120321639?thread_ts=1759444192.167409&cid=CJFHE7AR3). |

# Executive Summary

On September 26, 2025 at 19:00 UTC, Trino HMS query storms from ATS dashboard traffic overwhelmed the shared Hive Metastore infrastructure, causing both Trino query failures (ICEBERG\_INVALID\_METADATA errors) and ETL Spark job failures (HMS connection timeouts). The incident lasted more than 4 hours and was resolved by restarting Trino HMS Kubernetes pods.

**Root Cause**: The root cause is consistent with what we already know: too many partitions in some large hive tables, and we don’t have an effective way to limit the queries against those large partitions without hurting production workload.

The **proximate cause** is ATS dashboards with auto-refresh intervals as short as 56 seconds queried massive Hive tables (dw.clog\_by\_family: 20M partitions, dw.flog\_by\_api: 6.4M partitions) without partition filters. Trino HMS generated 194 queries examining **3.41 billion metadata rows (98% of total load)**. This caused HMS memory exhaustion and GC pauses (20-30 seconds on HMS on ETL, potentially much longer on HMS for Trino), creating a "slow consumer" problem where HMS couldn't receive RDS results fast enough, blocking RDS worker threads and cascading failures to EMR HMS instances.

HMS Configuration Flaw: Investigation revealed all three HMS instances/clusters had insufficient heap memory, some critical:

* **Trino HMS: 12 GB heap** \- Most critically undersized, could handle only 1 concurrent large query, caused primary bottleneck (98% of load)  
* **prod-etl-emr6 HMS: 24 GB heap** \- Undersized, could handle 2-3 queries, failed completely (122 apps killed)  
* **prod-dma-emr6 HMS: 36 GB heap** \- Barely adequate, could handle 3-5 queries, only survivor that was only impacted by the incident silently for one hour.

**Key Dashboards Identified**

* Dashboard 1000000001619018 (idavis): 880.6 CPU hours, 56-second auto-refresh, created 193 large queries that spanned 7-hour window, contributing to 54.9% of total load from ATS alone.  
* Dashboards from sswensen, mkhuat, folutade, seth.moore with 55-464 second planning times.

# Root Cause

## Primary Cause: Trino HMS Query Storm from ATS Dashboards

### *What Happened*

* 194 Trino HMS queries examined **3.41 billion HMS metadata rows (98% of total load)**  
* Tables: **`dw.clog_by_family`** (20M partitions), **`dw.flog_by_api`** (6.4M partitions)  
* Each query: 40M metadata rows, 60-65 seconds execution time  
* Source: 5 ATS dashboards with auto-refresh (56-90 second intervals)  
* First offending query: 15:51 UTC (3h before user reports)

### *Why It Overwhelmed the System*

1. **HMS Memory Exhaustion**: 40M-row result sets require 8-10 GB heap per query  
2. **GC Pauses**: prod-etl-emr6 (24 GB heap) experienced 20-30 second Stop-The-World GC pauses  
3. **Slow Consumer Problem**: During GC, HMS frozen → cannot read from network socket → TCP flow control pauses RDS → RDS worker threads block on send() → 60-second queries become 80-100 seconds total  
4. **Cascading Failure**: Extended worker thread occupancy → MySQL query queue grows → all HMS instances affected

EMR HMS instances generated 766 queries (80% by count) but only 64M metadata rows (2% of load). Most EMR queries hit cache and completed quickly (\<10 sec).Contributing Factor: HMS Configuration Disparity \- THE SMOKING GUN

| Cluster | HMS Heap | Concurrent Capacity | Outcome |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Trino HMS | 12 GB | 1 large query | PRIMARY BOTTLENECK (98% of load) |
| prod-etl-emr6 | 24 GB | 2-3 queries | Failed (122 apps killed) |
| prod-dma-emr6 | 36 GB | 3-5 queries | Survived (0% failure after hour 1\) |

## CRITICAL FINDING \- Trino HMS Heap Configuration

* **Discovered heap size: 12 GB** (from HADOOP\_HEAPSIZE=12288 in hive-env.sh)  
* **Kubernetes pod memory: 64 GB** (total container limit, includes JVM heap \+ overhead)  
* **Query memory requirements**: Each 40M-row partition query needs 8-10 GB heap  
* **Incident load**: 125 concurrent queries examining 3.41B metadata rows  
* **Heap deficit**: Needed 1,250 GB, had 12 GB \= 99% insufficient

### *Why This Is THE SMOKING GUN*

This discovery definitively connects both Trino and EMR HMS failures as stemming from insufficient heap memory across all HMS instances, with Trino HMS being the most critically undersized.

### *Cascade Failure Mechanism*

1. **Trino HMS (12 GB)** \- Most critically undersized \- Could handle: 1 concurrent large query \- Incident load: 125 concurrent queries (98% of total load) \- Result: Constant GC thrashing (Full GC every 7-10 seconds, 20-30 sec pauses) \- Impact: HMS frozen 60-75% of time → slow consumer problem → blocked RDS worker threads  
2. **prod-etl-emr6 (24 GB)** \- Secondary victim \- Could handle: 2-3 concurrent large queries \- Failed when Trino HMS load backed up RDS worker threads \- Result: 122 applications killed, unable to connect to HMS. **ETL users reported issues**.  
3. **prod-dma-emr6 (36 GB)** \- Only survivor \- Could handle: 3-5 concurrent large queries \- Adequate memory prevented GC thrashing \- Result: 5.5% → 0% failure rate with only one hour impact, users didn't complain.

### *Why Configuration Disparity Mattered*

* The 3x difference in heap size (12 GB → 24 GB → 36 GB) determined survival vs failure  
* Trino HMS's 12 GB heap made it the **weakest link**  
* prod-dma-emr6's 36 GB heap was the **only configuration with adequate capacity**  
* This **validates the "slow consumer" hypothesis**: insufficient heap → GC pauses → TCP flow control → blocked RDS worker threads

### *Why Restarting Trino HMS Fixed Both Issues*

Killed 194 Trino HMS queries (20% of query count, 98% of load) → freed RDS worker threads → cleared MySQL query queue → eliminated slow consumers → EMR HMS recovered. The system was at a tipping point; removing 98% of load restored stable operation. Immediately after restarting Trino HMS, we see a spike of S3 503 Slowdown (S3 rate limiting), signaling many Iceberg queries (from Trino HMS) which consume a lot less HMS memory, were able to proceed and land their request on S3.

# Critical Actions

## Reduce Hive partition counts

* We already know this, and we need to expedite the resolution of this root cause.   
* Most urgently, for these two tables impacting this incident: dw.clog\_by\_family, dw.flog\_by\_api.

## Optimize the identified ATS Dashboards

* Contact idavis (dashboard 1000000001619018): Increase refresh from 56s to 10+ min  
* Contact 4 other users (sswensen, mkhuat, folutade, seth.moore): Optimize queries with 55-464 second planning times  
* Expected impact: 70-80% load reduction

## Increase HMS Heap memory to at least 36G

* Increase HMS for all three impacted clusters: Trino HMS, HMS on ETL, and HMS on DMA.  
* Timeline: immediately (urgent change that should happen within next few days)

## Implement Query Rate Limiting

* Limit to 10 queries/second per connection  
* Prevents cache miss storm  
* Needs discussion if this is possible.

## Trino HMS Cluster (brainstorm)

* Do we have a Pod Disruption Budget to prevent simultaneous pod restarts (cache invalidation), or is this a concern? It appears that the post were not restarted during the incident, but that also means the long GC pause dragged the incident window for hours.  
* Can we add GC logs and persist them to S3? Logstash is not useful for RCA  
* Needs discussion if this is possible.  
* Now we know the top 5 ATS dashboard and how many partitions they are loading, can we figure out an exact limit to configure HMS to block those large queries? 

## Monitoring

* HMS GC pause monitoring (alert on \>10 seconds)  
* Slow query rate monitoring (alert on 5x baseline)  
* Cross-cluster HMS health dashboard  
* Enable performance insights on RDS (would show worker thread contention)

# Key Insights

1. **Query Count is Misleading**: Trino HMS had 20% of queries but 98% of actual load (metadata rows examined). Focus on load impact, not query count. **Simpson's Paradox\!**  
2. **Slow Consumer Problem**: HMS GC pauses create TCP flow control backpressure on RDS, blocking worker threads. Low network I/O (0.4%) was evidence of problem, not absence of problem.  
3. **Memory \> Connection Pools**: prod-dma-emr6 (10 connections, 36GB heap) survived; prod-etl-emr6 (30 connections, 24GB heap) failed. HMS heap must match query result set sizes.  
4. **Silent Failures Dangerous**: prod-dma-emr6 contributed 51% of queries but users didn't complain (long-running jobs, low visibility). Implement cross-cluster monitoring.  
5. **Dashboard Traffic Needs Different Planning**: User-facing dashboards with auto-refresh require query timeouts, rate limiting, and complexity guards that batch ETL doesn't need.

# Timeline

## Sept 26, 2025

* **15:51 UTC**: First offending dashboard query (464 sec planning time)  
* **16:00 UTC**: HMS load increases 4-6x baseline  
* **19:00 UTC**: Incident peak \- ETL timeouts, Trino errors (8-10x baseline load)  
* **20:00 UTC**: DI team restarts Trino HMS, kills 194 queries  
* **21:00 UTC**: Both issues resolved

**Background Context**: Sept 24 job delays (5-6x execution slowness, root cause unknown) created backlog leading to high concurrency on Sept 26\. Separate investigation needed

# Conclusion

Trino HMS query storms from ATS dashboards (98% of load) overwhelmed shared HMS infrastructure through HMS memory exhaustion and slow consumer problems. Configuration disparity (24GB vs 36GB heap) determined which clusters failed. Restarting Trino HMS removed the primary bottleneck, allowing system recovery.

# Appendix A: Detailed Evidence

## A.1 Query Distribution (Sept 26, 19:00-22:00 UTC)

| Source HMS | Queries | % Total | Metadata Rows | % Load |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Trino HMS | 194 | 20% | 3.41B | 98% |
| prod-dma-emr6 HMS | 494 | 51% | 36.9M | 1.1% |
| prod-etl-emr6 HMS | 265 | 28% | 0\* | 0% |
| prod-data-platform HMS | 7 | 1% | 26.9M | 0.8% |
| TOTAL | 960 | 100% | 3.48B | 100% |

\*prod-etl-emr6 had 0 slow queries (\>10s) during peak due to cache hits

## A.2 Hour-by-Hour Slow Query Pattern

```
Time (UTC) Queries Pattern
════════════════════════════════════════
Sept 26, 09:00 2-3 BaselineSept 26, 10:00 3-4 Baseline...Sept 26, 16:00 13-17 ⚠️ RISING (4-6x)Sept 26, 17:00 9-11 ⚠️ ELEVATEDSept 26, 18:00 15-19 ⚠️ HIGH (5-7x)Sept 26, 19:00 16-21 🔴 PEAK (8x)Sept 26, 20:00 20-26 🔴 HIGHEST (10x)Sept 26, 21:00 3-4 ✅ RECOVERY
```

Pattern: Acute overload, not chronic degradation. Normal baseline for 10+ hours before the incident.

## A.3 Top Offending Dashboards

| Dashboard ID | User | Loads | CPU Hrs | Refresh Interval | Impact |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1000000001619018 | idavis | 193 | 880.6 | 56 sec | 54.9% of load |
| 1000000001783686 | sswensen | 24 | 28.3 | \- | 58.5s avg planning |
| 1000000001707265 | mkhuat | 62 | 32.0 | 15 sec | Multi-tile burst |
| 1000000001809440 | folutade | 1 | \- | \- | 464s worst query |
| 1000000001599954 | seth.moore | 20 | 15.6 | \- | 55.7s avg planning |

Confirmed auto-refresh pattern: Dashboard 1000000001707265 loaded every 90 seconds during incident.

## A.4 RDS Metrics (Sept 26, 19:00-21:00 UTC)

**Hardware Metrics (appeared healthy)**:

* Connection utilization: 59% (1,473/2,500) \- NOT exhausted  
* CPU: 2-5% \- NOT saturated  
* Memory: 64 GB free \- adequate  
* Network I/O: 0.4% utilized (2-6 MB/s vs 1,500 MB/s capacity)  
* Disk I/O: Essentially 0 ReadIOPS (Aurora reads from memory)

**Application-Level Metrics (actual bottleneck)**:

* Slow queries: 960 queries, 60-65 seconds each  
* Expected: 10-15 seconds (4-6x slower)  
* Worker threads: \~125/200 occupied by slow Trino queries  
* Queue depth: 150+ queries waiting (estimated)

**Why metrics misleading**: Hardware idle but worker threads blocked waiting for slow HMS consumers (GC pauses).

## A.5 S3 Rate Limiting Metrics

Right after the Trino HMS was restarted, we see a large spike of S3 Rate Limiting (503 SlowDown), an evidence that after the restart, the long-running Hive queries are not causing the Trino HMS long GC pause, which allowed Iceberg queries to generate large volume of Iceberg metadata file access. 

![][image1]

# Appendix B: Ruled Out Hypotheses

## B.1. foundations\_performance jobs

Initial Hypothesis: Long-running jobs (33.5 hours) caused HMS overload.  
Investigation Findings:

* Jobs queried icekone.traces (Iceberg table), NOT Hive tables  
* Iceberg uses S3 manifests, minimal HMS load (\<1 query/min)  
* YARN resource contention: NONE (100% success rate during runtime)  
* Timing: Incident peaked 3.5 hours AFTER jobs terminated

Conclusion: RULED OUT as incident cause. Separate issue (Iceberg file proliferation: 2.78M files).

## B.2. September 24 Execution Delays

Initial Hypothesis: Sept 24 job delays caused Sept 26 HMS overload.  
Investigation Findings:

* Sept 24 20:00 UTC: Jobs took 5-6x longer to execute (root cause unknown)  
* Created backlog leading to 59 concurrent jobs on Sept 26  
* Connection to Sept 26 HMS overload: Unclear

Conclusion: SEPARATE ISSUE requiring independent investigation. Created background pressure but not direct cause of HMS overload.

## B.3. RDS Resource Exhaustion

Initial Hypothesis: RDS connection pool or CPU exhaustion.  
Investigation Findings:

* Connections: 59% utilized (NOT exhausted)  
* CPU: 2-5% (NOT saturated)  
* Memory: 64 GB free (adequate)  
* Network: 0.4% utilized (NOT saturated)  
* Disk I/O: 0 ReadIOPS (NOT bottleneck)

Conclusion: RULED OUT. Bottleneck was HMS application-level (GC pauses, slow consumer problem), not RDS hardware capacity.

# Appendix C: Technical Architecture

## C.1 System Architecture

![][image2]

## C.2. Failure Mechanism

```
1. ATS Dashboard Auto-Refresh (56-90 sec intervals)   ↓2. Trino HMS: 194 queries × 40M rows each = 3.41B rows   ↓3. HMS Heap Pressure: 12GB heap, 8-10GB per query	⚠️  CRITICAL: 12GB can only handle 1 query, but 125 concurrent	⚠️  Heap deficit: Needed 1,250 GB, had 12 GB = 99% insufficient   ↓4. GC Pause: Constant Full GC (every 7-10 sec), 20-30 sec pauses   ↓5. Slow Consumer: HMS frozen 60-75% of time → TCP flow control pauses RDS   ↓6. RDS Worker Threads: Block on send(), occupy threads 80-100 sec   ↓7. MySQL Query Queue: Grows to 150+ queries   ↓8. EMR HMS Queries: Timeout waiting in queue   ↓9. prod-etl-emr6 HMS (24 GB): Also experiences GC pressure   ↓10. Spark Jobs: Fail to initialize (HMS unreachable)   ↓11. Positive Feedback Loop: Retries → more HMS load
```

## C.3. Slow Consumer Problem Explained

Normal flow (no GC): RDS query (10s) \+ send (2s) \= 12s total  
With HMS GC pause:

* RDS query (60s) \+ blocked send during HMS GC (20s) \= 80s total  
* Worker thread occupied 80s instead of 12s  
* Low network I/O (0.4%) is evidence HMS couldn't receive faster

## C.4. Why Iceberg Query Failed

Trino queries HMS for table location, then reads S3 manifests:

```
Trino query → Trino HMS (get table S3 location) → HMS overloaded/GC pause → Timeout → Error: ICEBERG_INVALID_METADATA (misleading)
```
